Shopify–eBay Integration: From Legacy System to Built for Shopify Compliance

How I led a full design system migration from Ounce to Shopify Polaris, resolved 30+ UX and content gaps, and modernised a complex marketplace integration app to achieve Built for Shopify readiness.

Design Systems BFS Compliance Shopify Polaris Lead Product Designer Threecolts UX Audit
Built for Shopify Redesign

Shopify–eBay Integration

Design Systems Migration · UX Compliance · Polaris

My Role
Lead Product Designer
Scope
End-to-end redesign
System
Shopify Polaris
Team
PMs, Devs, QA
Outcome
BFS readiness achieved
01 · Executive Summary

A compliance-critical redesign that went far deeper than a visual refresh.

The Shopify–eBay integration app at Threecolts was a functionally capable product sitting on a legacy design system that was increasingly incompatible with the platform it lived inside. As Shopify raised the bar for Built for Shopify certification, the gap between what the app looked like and what Shopify expected became a real business risk.

I led the end-to-end product design for this initiative — from a comprehensive UX audit through to full Polaris migration across eight core modules. This was not a visual refresh. It was a product maturity initiative that touched design systems, information architecture, content strategy, accessibility, and merchant onboarding in equal measure.

Business stakes: Built for Shopify is not just a badge. It is a primary trust signal for merchants evaluating apps in the Shopify App Store. Failing to meet BFS standards meant limited discoverability, lower merchant confidence, and a product that felt foreign inside the Shopify admin — directly impacting adoption and retention.
02 · The Problem

A product built to work, but not built for Shopify.

The app had been designed using Ounce — a legacy custom design system — at a time when BFS standards were less defined. Over time, the product accrued significant design debt. Each module had grown independently, creating a fragmented experience that was inconsistent internally and misaligned with Shopify's admin patterns externally.

🧩

Design system fragmentation

Ounce components were incompatible with Polaris. Buttons, typography, spacing, and colour usage varied across modules, breaking visual and behavioural consistency throughout the app.

🚫

BFS compliance gaps

Multiple modules failed Shopify's design quality expectations — from non-Polaris button hierarchies and poor spacing to missing empty states and weak error handling.

🗺️

Fragmented information architecture

Navigation patterns did not align with Shopify admin conventions. Merchants familiar with the Shopify ecosystem found the app's structure unintuitive and disorienting.

📝

Content and accessibility issues

CTAs were ambiguous, instructional copy was inconsistent, and WCAG contrast requirements were not met in several areas — all BFS rejection criteria.

🔄

Onboarding friction

The onboarding flow did not follow Shopify's expected setup patterns, creating confusion at the most critical moment in the merchant journey.

📉

Merchant trust erosion

An app that looks different from the Shopify admin creates immediate doubt. The visual disconnect signalled to merchants that this was a third-party bolt-on — not a trusted integration.

03 · Why Built for Shopify Matters

BFS is not a checkbox. It is a product quality standard.

Built for Shopify is Shopify's highest tier of app certification. It signals to merchants that an app meets Shopify's own standards for design quality, performance, and security. For an integration app operating inside the Shopify admin, it is the difference between feeling like a native tool and feeling like a foreign intrusion.

What BFS requires: Polaris-native component usage, App Bridge integration, predictable navigation, card-based layouts, accessible colour contrast, clear onboarding, responsive design, CTA clarity, and content consistency — all evaluated through a formal Shopify review process.
Business consequence of BFS failure

Apps without BFS certification are deprioritised in the Shopify App Store search algorithm, excluded from Shopify's curated collections, and carry a visible trust deficit compared to certified alternatives. For a marketplace integration app competing on discoverability, this was not acceptable.

Beyond certification, Polaris alignment delivers a compounding benefit: merchants who already know the Shopify admin require zero relearning. Every familiar pattern reduces cognitive load, shortens onboarding, and increases the likelihood of successful setup and long-term retention.

04 · Goals and Success Criteria

Six outcomes that defined what success looked like.

🎯

Polaris migration

Complete replacement of Ounce components with Shopify Polaris equivalents across all eight modules.

✅

BFS compliance readiness

Resolve all audit findings that would trigger a Shopify review rejection — design, content, and accessibility.

🧭

Navigation standardisation

Restructure the app's information architecture to align with Shopify admin patterns merchants already know.

🚀

Onboarding simplification

Redesign the setup flow to reduce time-to-value and eliminate the primary sources of merchant abandonment.

♿

Accessibility compliance

Meet WCAG 2.1 AA requirements across all modules — a non-negotiable BFS prerequisite.

📐

Modular scalability

Build on a design system foundation that supports future feature growth without accumulating new design debt.

05 · My Role and Ownership

Lead designer, auditor, and design systems migration owner.

This was a project with significant scope and ambiguity. There was no prior design documentation, no component inventory, and no formal BFS gap analysis. I built all of that from scratch while simultaneously designing the solutions.

→

UX Audit Lead

Conducted a heuristic analysis across all eight modules, benchmarked against Shopify guidelines, and created the 30+ issue framework used to prioritise the redesign.

→

Design System Migration

Owned the Ounce to Polaris component mapping, design token standardisation, and the governance decisions around how Polaris patterns were adapted for marketplace-specific complexity.

→

Information Architecture

Restructured the navigation and module hierarchy to align with Shopify admin conventions while preserving the business logic of a complex integration workflow.

→

Cross-functional Collaboration

Worked directly with PMs on scope prioritisation, engineering on implementation constraints, QA on BFS validation criteria, and content on copy standardisation.

06 · Discovery and UX Audit

30+ issues. One framework. A clear path to BFS readiness.

Before designing anything, I conducted a comprehensive audit of the existing product. The goal was to move beyond subjective observations and create a structured, prioritised view of exactly what stood between the current state and BFS certification.

The audit covered heuristic analysis, Shopify guideline benchmarking, BFS gap analysis, content review, accessibility scanning, navigation assessment, and component inventory. Every issue was categorised by severity and module.

Audit methodology

Issues were classified as Critical (BFS rejection risk), Major (significant UX degradation), or Minor (content and polish). This triage directly determined design sprint sequencing and engineering handoff priority.

Critical

Non-Polaris button hierarchy

Primary and secondary actions used Ounce styling not recognised by Shopify's review system as compliant.

Critical

Missing empty and error states

Multiple modules displayed blank screens or unformatted error messages when data was absent — a direct BFS rejection trigger.

Critical

Accessibility contrast failures

Several text-on-background combinations failed WCAG 2.1 AA contrast ratios — mandatory for BFS certification.

Major

Inconsistent spacing system

Spacing values were non-standardised across modules, creating visual noise and undermining the perception of product quality.

Major

Ambiguous CTA language

Action labels like "Submit", "Go" and "Continue" lacked clarity about what would happen next — increasing merchant hesitation at key decision points.

Major

Navigation misalignment

The app used sidebar patterns inconsistent with Shopify admin, creating disorientation for merchants accustomed to Shopify's navigation conventions.

Minor

Typography mismatch

Heading levels and font weights were inconsistently applied, weakening information hierarchy across key merchant workflows.

Minor

Poor mobile responsiveness

Several layouts broke or became unusable on smaller viewports — expected behaviour for Shopify embedded apps.

Minor

Content inconsistency

Terminology varied across modules — "listing" vs "product", "sync" vs "upload" — creating confusion in a workflow where precise language matters.

07 · Major Challenges

The hardest parts were not design problems. They were system problems.

Challenge 1: Migrating without breaking

The app was live and actively used by merchants. The redesign had to be delivered in phases without disrupting existing workflows or triggering unexpected behaviour. Every Polaris component swap required validation against the underlying business logic it was replacing.

Challenge 2: Polaris adaptation for marketplace complexity

Polaris is optimised for standard e-commerce admin tasks. A marketplace integration app — with product sync, template logic, pricing rules, and error resolution — required careful adaptation. Using Polaris patterns directly sometimes created layouts that were technically compliant but functionally inadequate for the data density the app required.

Challenge 3: Merchant familiarity vs design modernisation

Existing merchants had built mental models around the legacy interface. Significant structural changes — even positive ones — risked disorientation. Every architectural decision required weighing BFS compliance against the disruption cost to existing users.

Challenge 4: Engineering implementation constraints

Some Polaris components had implementation dependencies that conflicted with the app's existing React architecture. Several design decisions had to be revised after engineering feasibility reviews, requiring pragmatic compromises that preserved the spirit of Polaris compliance without demanding a full technical rewrite.

08 · Strategic Design Approach

Polaris first. Merchant clarity second. Everything else follows.

The design strategy was built on a clear hierarchy: compliance unlocks discoverability, consistency builds trust, and clarity drives adoption. Every decision was evaluated against this sequence before anything reached high-fidelity.

Core principle: Polaris is not a constraint — it is a communication strategy. When merchants see familiar components, they spend cognitive energy on tasks rather than on understanding the interface. Every Polaris-native pattern we adopted was a reduction in onboarding friction.
🏗️

Component-first migration

Built a complete Ounce-to-Polaris component mapping before touching any module layout. This ensured consistency and gave engineering a predictable migration path rather than a moving target.

📐

Design token standardisation

Replaced ad-hoc spacing, colour, and typography values with Polaris tokens throughout. This single change resolved the majority of spacing and hierarchy audit findings across all modules simultaneously.

🗂️

Card-based layout restructuring

Reorganised module layouts around Polaris card patterns — the BFS-expected structure for grouping related information. This improved scannability and immediately aligned the visual rhythm with the Shopify admin.

🧭

Navigation simplification

Restructured the app navigation to follow App Bridge patterns, reducing the number of navigation levels and making the app feel embedded in Shopify rather than layered on top of it.

✍️

Content redesign

Rewrote CTAs, instructional copy, and empty states across all modules using Shopify's content guidelines as the standard. Every piece of text was evaluated for clarity, tone, and action specificity.

♿

Accessibility upgrades

Resolved all WCAG contrast failures, added proper focus states, and ensured keyboard navigability — moving from audit findings to testable compliance in each module before handoff.

09 · Module-by-Module Breakdown

Eight modules. One cohesive system.

The redesign covered the full product surface — eight modules, each with distinct complexity and distinct BFS compliance challenges. Below are the four highest-impact modules.

01

Onboarding

The highest-stakes module. Merchants who struggle here rarely come back. The legacy flow front-loaded complexity, asked for information before establishing value, and provided no progress feedback — all BFS red flags.

Legacy issues
  • No progress indication
  • Account connection buried in step 3
  • Error states displayed inline without guidance
  • Non-Polaris form components throughout
Redesign approach
  • Polaris Setup Guide pattern adopted
  • Progressive disclosure — surface only what's needed
  • Account connection moved to step 1
  • Inline validation with specific, actionable error copy
BFS compliance outcome
  • Shopify-native setup pattern
  • Clear progress visibility
  • Accessible error handling
  • Reduced setup abandonment
Complete onboarding flow — 4 steps
1 Pre-requisites checklist
2 Connect eBay account
✓ Account authorised successfully
3 Import Shopify products
4 Pick the right plan
✓ Payment approved — setup complete
02

Dashboard

The post-login landing experience was a wall of data with no hierarchy. Merchants could not quickly understand their sync status, pending actions, or listing health — the three things they come to the dashboard for.

Legacy issues
  • Flat layout, no card hierarchy
  • Status indicators non-standard
  • No empty state for new merchants
  • Critical actions buried below fold
Redesign approach
  • Polaris card hierarchy introduced
  • Status badges standardised to Polaris Badge
  • Empty states designed for all data-absent scenarios
  • Primary actions surfaced in consistent positions
BFS compliance outcome
  • Reduced cognitive load
  • Polaris-native layout pattern
  • Clear merchant orientation
  • Improved visual hierarchy
Dashboard screens

Dashboard overview with products, orders, and sales data

Activities log with ongoing sync status and completed actions

Contact Us modal — schedule a meeting or raise a concern

Help and Support with searchable FAQ and module-based categories

03

Templates

Templates are the most operationally complex module — they govern how product data maps to eBay listing requirements. The legacy UX made this complexity visible in all the wrong ways, overwhelming merchants with options before they understood the purpose of each field.

Legacy issues
  • No section grouping or hierarchy
  • Bulk action patterns non-standard
  • Ambiguous field labels throughout
  • No inline guidance for complex inputs
Redesign approach
  • Polaris ResourceList for template management
  • Collapsible sections to reduce visible complexity
  • Standardised bulk actions using Polaris patterns
  • Inline help text added to all complex fields
BFS compliance outcome
  • Shopify-native list management
  • Reduced cognitive overload
  • Scalable to growing template libraries
  • Content clarity across all fields
Templates screen

Templates list view with category, inventory, price and title template types — filterable and searchable

04

Pricing

Pricing rules are high-stakes — a misconfigured rule can result in incorrect pricing across hundreds of listings. The legacy interface did not communicate pricing logic clearly, provided no confirmation feedback, and used custom form components that were not Polaris-compliant.

Legacy issues
  • Pricing rule logic not explained in-context
  • No confirmation on save
  • Custom input components non-compliant
  • No error prevention for invalid rule combinations
Redesign approach
  • Live preview of pricing rule effect
  • Polaris TextField and Select components adopted
  • Inline validation prevents invalid combinations
  • Success toast on save for confirmation feedback
BFS compliance outcome
  • Merchant confidence in rule configuration
  • Fully Polaris-compliant form pattern
  • Error prevention over error recovery
  • Clear feedback on all save actions
Pricing plans screen

Pricing Plans — tiered plan selection with monthly/yearly toggle, feature comparison, and custom solutions for high-volume merchants

05

Product Listing

The product management surface is where merchants spend most of their operational time. The legacy view lacked clear status indicators, bulk action patterns were non-standard, and variant data was buried — making it hard to act on listing errors at scale.

Legacy issues
  • Status badges non-Polaris compliant
  • Variant data not surfaced at list level
  • Bulk actions buried and inconsistent
  • No quick-assign profile option
Redesign approach
  • Polaris IndexTable with standardised badges
  • Inline variant count with expandable detail
  • Bulk Update and Export CSV surfaced as primary actions
  • Profile assignment inline with status visibility
BFS compliance outcome
  • Shopify-native table and badge patterns
  • Reduced clicks for common actions
  • Clear error and status visibility at scale
  • Accessible row interactions
Product listing screen

Manage Products view showing product status badges, variant counts, profile assignment, and multi-account filtering

Other Screens

Additional modules redesigned.

Beyond the core modules, the redesign covered Orders, Profiling, Configuration, and other supporting surfaces — each migrated to Polaris and audited for BFS compliance. Below are three additional screens showing the depth of the redesign.

Orders — eBay order management with real-time sync status, multi-account filtering, and Polaris status badges

Create Profile — profile creation with multi-account selection, business policy mapping, template assignments, and product assignment rules

Account Settings — multi-region eBay account management with reconnect toggles, account details, and customer service metrics

10 · BFS Compliance Achieved

Every review criterion addressed.

Shopify's Built for Shopify review evaluates apps against a defined set of design, content, and accessibility criteria. By the end of the redesign, every criterion that had been flagged as a gap in the audit was resolved.

✓
Polaris component usage throughout
✓
App Bridge embedded navigation patterns
✓
Card-based layout structure
✓
Clear, progressive onboarding flow
✓
WCAG 2.1 AA contrast compliance
✓
Keyboard navigability across all modules
✓
Empty states for all data-absent scenarios
✓
Actionable, specific error messages
✓
Consistent CTA language and hierarchy
✓
Responsive mobile-friendly layouts
✓
Content consistency across all modules
✓
Standardised spacing via Polaris tokens
11 · Design Process

Eight stages. Structured to move fast without accumulating new debt.

A deliberate sequence from uncovering problems to delivering a system built to last.

01
🔍

Audit

Heuristic and BFS gap analysis across all eight modules

02
📊

Prioritise

Issue triage by severity, BFS risk, and merchant impact

03
🗺️

Map

Ounce to Polaris component mapping and IA restructure

04
✏️

Wireframe

Low-fidelity flows validated with PMs before visual work

05
🎨

Design

High-fidelity Polaris-native screens per module

06
👥

Review

Stakeholder alignment and BFS criteria cross-check

07
🧪

QA

Design-QA alignment on implementation accuracy

08
📦

Handoff

Annotated specs and component documentation delivered

12 · Cross-functional Collaboration

Compliance redesigns do not happen in design tools alone.

The BFS requirement introduced stakeholders who are not typical in a standard product redesign. QA teams needed to understand what "Polaris compliance" meant in test terms. Engineering needed component-level guidance, not just screens. PMs needed scope prioritisation that balanced BFS urgency against development capacity. Content needed clear guidelines on Shopify's tone and terminology standards.

Key collaboration outcome: By running weekly cross-functional reviews throughout the redesign — not just at handoff — we identified implementation blockers, content gaps, and QA ambiguities early enough to resolve them within the design phase rather than during development.
13 · Outcomes and Product Impact

What changed for the product, the team, and the merchants.

🏆

BFS readiness achieved

All 30+ audit findings resolved. The app met the design, content, and accessibility criteria required for Built for Shopify certification review — unlocking App Store prioritisation.

🧩

Unified design system

Ounce replaced entirely with Polaris. Consistent components, tokens, and patterns across all eight modules — eliminating the visual fragmentation that had accumulated over years of independent growth.

🚀

Streamlined onboarding

Setup flow redesigned around Shopify-native patterns, reducing the steps required to reach first successful listing sync and removing the primary sources of merchant abandonment.

🤝

Merchant trust improved

An app that looks and behaves like a native Shopify tool signals quality and reliability. The Polaris migration directly addressed the perception gap between the app and the admin it lived inside.

📐

Scalable foundation

The Polaris-based design system can support future module additions without accumulating new design debt. New features inherit the design language automatically rather than requiring case-by-case decisions.

⚡

Faster design and development

Polaris components reduced design-to-handoff time for future features. Engineering could reference documented Polaris patterns rather than interpreting custom component behaviour from scratch.

14 · Key Learnings

What this project taught me about designing inside ecosystems.

01

Design systems compliance is product strategy

Adopting Polaris was not a design decision — it was a business decision. Platform-native design systems carry trust, discoverability, and development velocity benefits that go far beyond visual consistency. Treating design system alignment as a strategic lever, not a constraint, changes how you prioritise and communicate the work.

02

Audit before redesign — always

Starting with a structured audit rather than jumping to solutions changed the quality of every subsequent decision. The 30+ issue framework gave the team a shared vocabulary, prevented scope creep, and made the ROI of the redesign visible to non-design stakeholders in concrete terms.

03

Platform-native UX reduces onboarding cost at scale

Every Polaris pattern we adopted was a learning that merchants did not have to do. In an integration app where the setup process is already complex, reducing interface learning to zero is a meaningful product advantage. Familiarity is a feature.

04

Compliance and usability are not in tension — they reinforce each other

The assumption going in was that BFS compliance would require trade-offs against merchant usability. It did not. In almost every case, the Polaris pattern was also the clearer, more usable solution. BFS requirements pointed toward better UX, not away from it.

05

If I were doing this again

I would involve engineering earlier in the component mapping phase. Several late-stage design revisions were triggered by implementation feasibility issues that would have been surfaced — and solved — earlier with more engineering input at the audit and mapping stage.

The takeaway

This was not a UI refresh. It was a product maturity initiative — a design systems migration, a compliance transformation, and a merchant experience overhaul delivered in a single cohesive effort. The outcome was an app that finally felt like it belonged inside Shopify, because it was built to Shopify's own standards.

Mariya Khan · Senior Product Designer · 2025
